title: Cardiac/venous return curves: decreased contractility (MI)
vignette: The cardiac output and venous return curves of a healthy person are shown below with solid lines. The dashed lines show a decreased cardiac output (cardiac function) curve with an unchanged venous return curve. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the change depicted by the dashed lines?

clues.note: A downward-shifted cardiac function curve means reduced contractility for any given preload (right atrial pressure). An isolated drop in the cardiac function curve indicates a negative inotropic state or myocardial injury.

If the venous return curve is UNCHANGED, then blood volume and total peripheral resistance/venous tone are normal (mean systemic filling pressure unchanged). So the problem is not volume or vascular - it's an isolated contractility defect.

whatMatters: The dashed lines show a depressed cardiac function curve with an UNCHANGED venous return curve. That isolates the defect to decreased contractility (not changed preload or afterload). The cause is myocardial injury - a myocardial infarction - answer D.
strategy: Decode the two-curve plot: the cardiac function (CO) curve reflects contractility; the venous return curve reflects blood volume and venous tone (its x-intercept is mean systemic filling pressure). A pure drop in the CO curve with an unchanged VR curve = isolated negative inotropy/myocardial damage. Volume/vascular changes would shift the VR curve.

eliminations.label: excessive hydration - shifts venous return curve right
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: volume overload increases mean systemic filling pressure. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it shifts the VENOUS RETURN curve rightward (higher x-intercept), but the VR curve here is unchanged. ✅ Correct if: the venous return curve had moved right with higher filling pressure.

eliminations.label: acute hemorrhage - shifts venous return curve left
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: blood loss decreases mean systemic filling pressure. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it shifts the VR curve LEFTWARD (lower x-intercept); but the VR curve is unchanged and the CO curve dropped, so it's not volume loss. ✅ Correct if: the VR curve had shifted left.

eliminations.label: chronic anemia - increases cardiac output curve
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: reduced blood viscosity and high-output compensation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: chronic anemia tends to INCREASE cardiac output and venous return (raise the curves), the opposite of a depressed CO curve. ✅ Correct if: both curves were elevated (high-output state).

eliminations.label: anaphylaxis - decreases venous return (vasodilation/third-spacing)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: massive vasodilation and capillary leak. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it lowers mean systemic filling pressure and shifts the VR curve, rather than isolatedly depressing the CO curve; here VR is unchanged. ✅ Correct if: the VR curve had fallen from venodilation/volume loss.

reasoning: Cardiac function (CO) curve shifted DOWN = decreased contractility
Venous return curve UNCHANGED = normal volume and venous tone
Isolated negative inotropy points to myocardial damage
mechanism: The Guyton cardiac output/venous return diagram plots two relationships against right atrial pressure: the cardiac function curve (how cardiac output rises with preload, reflecting contractility/Frank-Starling) and the venous return curve (how venous return falls as right atrial pressure rises; its x-intercept is the mean systemic filling pressure, set by blood volume and venous tone). A downward shift of the cardiac function curve with an unchanged venous return curve indicates decreased contractility with normal volume and vascular tone - i.e., an intrinsic myocardial problem such as a myocardial infarction (or a negative inotropic drug). Changes in blood volume (hydration, hemorrhage) or vascular tone (anaphylaxis) would instead shift the venous return curve, and chronic anemia would raise output.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Physiology > Cardiac output & venous return (Guyton) curves: cardiac function curve = contractility (Frank-Starling); venous return curve x-intercept = mean systemic filling pressure (volume + venous tone). Decreased contractility shifts CO curve down; volume/vascular changes shift VR curve.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Read which curve moved: only the CO curve dropped while VR is unchanged -> isolated contractility loss. Then choose the cause that does exactly that.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on cardiac function vs venous return curve, mean systemic filling pressure, contractility/inotropy, Frank-Starling? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The volume/vascular distractors (hydration, hemorrhage, anaphylaxis) all move the VENOUS RETURN curve, but it's unchanged here. Only an inotropy drop (MI) lowers the CO curve in isolation.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: On the graph: the dashed cardiac-output curve sits below the solid one; the venous-return line is identical -> isolated contractility defect.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: You picked D correctly - tie 'CO curve down, VR unchanged' to a contractility/MI problem.

keywords.front: Cardiac function curve shifts down, venous return unchanged

keywords.back: Mean systemic filling pressure (blood volume + venous tone)

keywords.front: What raises mean systemic filling pressure
keywords.back: Volume overload, increased venous tone (shifts VR curve right)

keywords.front: What lowers mean systemic filling pressure

memoryHook: CO curve sags but VR line holds = the pump is weak, not the tank. Weak pump = MI.
